title: ECG Authentication Hardware Design With Low-Power Signal Processing and Neural Network Optimization With Low Precision and Structured Compression
venue: IEEE Transactions on Biomedical Circuits and Systems

biometric.body_location: arms (right arm negative terminal, left arm positive terminal)

method.training_strategy: Authentication-specific cost function (minimizing intra-individual distance, maximizing inter-individual distance); joint optimization of 2-bit low-precision weights and coarse-grain structured sparsity (CGS).

evaluation.context: Achieved using 4X compressed NN and averaging 30 beats on the 741-subject in-house database.

evaluation_protocol: Cosine similarity comparison between registered ECG features and identification features; evaluated across different starting points in the ECG record.

key_contribution: Development of a low-power ASIC for ECG biometric authentication
Novel authentication-specific NN cost function to minimize EER
Hardware-friendly NN compression via joint low-precision and structured sparsity
main_strength: Effective scalability to a large subject pool (741 users)
High hardware efficiency (reduced SRAM footprint by 104X compared to floating-point)
main_weakness: No evaluation against active spoofing attacks
Evaluation limited to lab/in-house datasets without uncontrolled real-world testing
insights.notes_for_review: Focuses heavily on hardware efficiency (ASIC) rather than algorithmic novelty in signal processing, but introduces a specific cost function for authentication.
